rulings.Case Title: IN RE: JULIE SU
rulings.Calendar Matter: Order To Show Cause Hearing Re Administrative Subpoenas
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Friday, February 28, 2014, Line 6, Order To Show Cause Hearing Re Administrative Subpoenas. Pro Tem Judge Nils Rosenquest, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required on Order to Show Cause pursuant to Govt Code Sec. 11188. Moving party to bring proposed order to the hearing. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: GARY SCHEFSKY VS. SAN FRANCSICO TENNIS CLUB et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, February 28, 2014, Line 9, DEFENDANT WESTERN ATHLETIC CLUBS, INC.'s MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. Denied. Defendants have not met their initial burden to show that undisputed facts support each element of the affirmative defense. (Andersen v. Metalclad Insulation Corp. (1992) 72 Cal.App.4th 284, 289.) While the doctrine of assumption of the risk provides that there is no duty to eliminate risks that are inherent in a sport or activity, "unnecessarily dangerous equipment is not considered an inherent risk of a sport." (Zipusch v. LA Workout, Inc. (2007) 155 Cal.App.4th 1281, 1292.) Defendants have provided no evidence establishing how an Indo Board is properly used, and therefore, have not established that they provided the Indo Board for use to club members in such a way that allowed for proper usage. As such, Defendants have not established that the Indo Board was not "unnecessarily dangerous equipment." Even assuming that Defendants have met their burden in establishing that Plaintiff cannot establish breach of any duty because there was a label on the Indo Board that warned club members to use the Board at their own risk (Undisputed Material Fact, no. 9.), Plaintiff has provided evidence establishing there is a triable issue of material fact as to whether this warning was sufficient, given that placing the Board on a hard surface, as opposed to carpet, increased the risk of injury. (Additional Disputed Fact ("ADF"), no. 34.) Additionally, there is a triable issue of material fact as whether Defendant had a duty to furnish more information to club members using the Board for the first time. (ADF, no. 32-33.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/PJB)

rulings.Case Title: ROBERT SILL VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar1 for Friday, February 28, 2014, Line 16, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. Denied. CCSF has not met its initial burden. First, CCSF has not shown that Plaintiff cannot identify the specific toilet at issue. In any event, there is a triable issue of fact whether Plaintiff can do so. (UMF no. 2.) The evidence proffered does not support UMF no. 2. Second, Defendant CCSF failed to address all of the facts pled in the First Amended Complaint. Defendant CCSF focuses exclusively on the notice prong of the statute, and has not addressed the allegation that the condition was created by CCSF employees pursuant to Gov. Code § 835(a)-in which case notice would not be necessary. (First. Am. Complaint, p. 4, Prem.L-4b.) Therefore, even if CCSF had shown that Plaintiff could not prove notice (which the Court does not decide), CCSF has failed to show that Plaintiff cannot prevail on his claim. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/PJB)

Pro Tem Judge Nils Rosenquest, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Parties to meet and confer prior to hearing on time and place for scheduling of depositions in view of defendant's filing cross-complaint (Opposition, fn. 5). =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ANTOLYN ROSALES VS. SOCIAL VOCATIONAL SERVICES, INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, February 28, 2014, Line 18, DEFENDANT SOCIAL VOCATIONAL SERVICES, INC.'s DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Sustained, with 10 days leave to amend, as to the seventh cause of action for wrongful termination in violation of Gov. Code § 12940 if Plaintiff can plead in good faith a cognizable claim not already contained in the remaining discrimination and termination causes of action. There is no cognizable cause of action for wrongful termination in violation of Gov. Code § 12940. Overruled as to the 10th cause of action for negligent hiring, supervision, and retention, the 11th cause of action for negligence, and the 13th cause of action for negligent infliction of emotional distress. For the 10th, 11th and 13th causes of action, the exclusivity rule does not bar actions where the misconduct exceeds the normal risks of the employment relationship. Plaintiff has pled sufficient facts. Hine v. Dittrich (1991) 228 Cal.App.3d 59, 64 is distinguishable. In Hine, the plaintiff "specifically allege[d] he suffered no injury until he was discharged." (Hine v. Dittrich (1991) 228 Cal.App.3d 59, 64.) Here, Plaintiff alleges that she suffered injury before she was discharged. For the eleventh and thirteenth causes of action, Plaintiff has pled sufficient facts and may plead an alternate theory of negligence liability under a separate heading. Denied as to the motion to strike the prayer for punitive damages. Plaintiff has adequately pled facts to support a claim for punitive damages. As pled, Defendant employee's harassment and discrimination form the basis of oppression and malice, and Defendant allegedly ratified the acts by terminating Plaintiff. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/PJB)

rulings.Case Title: BRIAN GARRETT VS. ORIENT BANCORPORATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Petition And Petition To Compel Arbitration And Stay Judicial Proceedings
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, February 28, 2014, Line 20, DEFENDANT ORIENT BANCORPORATION, BANK OF THE ORIENT's Petition To Compel Arbitration And Stay Judicial Proceedings. Granted. Defendants' request for judicial notice is granted. Plaintiff does not dispute that the complaint comes within the scope of the Arbitration Agreement or that the signed Arbitration Agreement meets the Armendariz v. Foundation Health Psychcare Services, Inc. (2000) 24 Cal.4th 83, factors. (Declaration of Shew-Woo In Support of Petition, 6-8, Exhs. A-C.) Plaintiff has not carried his burden of demonstrating the Arbitration Agreement is unconscionable. Even assuming that the limitation of the scope of the matters to be arbitrated introduces some relatively minor degree of substantive unconscionability by focusing on claims most likely to be raised by an employee, Plaintiff has not demonstrated procedural unconscionability-or at least the degree of procedural unconscionability necessary to render the Arbitration Agreement unenforceable in light of evidence concerning the parties' relative bargaining power, the course of negotiations over terms of employment, and the fact that Plaintiff was alerted to the arbitration requirement in the offer letter. The proceedings are stayed until arbitration is completed. (CCP § 1281.4.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order and/or statement of decision with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page and must bring the proposed order and/or statement of decision to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/PJB)

rulings.Case Title: SATINDER SINGH VS. XUONG LU et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Moiton And Moiton In Support Of Petition For Order Relieving Petition From Gvt Code 945.4
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, February 28, 2014, Line 23, PLAINTIFF SATINDER SINGH's Motion In Support Of Petition For Order Relieving Petition From Government Code Section 945.4. Granted. (CCP § 946.6(c)(1).) Plaintiff has demonstrated that the failure to present a timely claim under Gov't Code § 945.4 was the result of surprise or excusable neglect. Plaintiff was unaware of CCSF's involvement in the subject accident until 7/03/13 when Defendant Reed's insurer, York Risk Services, informed him that Mr. Reed may have been driving the vehicle during the course and scope of his employment with CCSF. Once informed, Plaintiff acted diligently, and CCSF has not sufficiently demonstrated any prejudice. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests.
